speaker: interviewer
text: 9Th of October, 1995, Henry Kinsley, tape two. You were saying about that you went at a town near [NON-ENGLISH], to your mother's town. What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radomysl Wielki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-D-O-M-Y-S-L capital W-I-E-L-K-I. It's a small town, you know, the market, around the market were houses all around. That's for the whole town. And we stayed over there, because my mother comes from over there. They got a house that's from her parents. We stayed over there almost-- almost a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what time to what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Krakow-- what time? From 1942 to 1943, something like this. And I was over there, my wife, my parents, and my two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 19-- wait a minute-- 1940, in August, I think, in Krakow. And-- and she was with me all the time until liberation, even came to America with me, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your family were all together in this little town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of other Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father got some kind of idea. He made-- during the war, you know, there was no soap. You know? So he got some chemicals, and something, and he made soap. And we used to sell it. You know, I'm very careful, because a German catch you with this, you'll not go to Siberia, you know. So we did this here. And the same thing, from savings, something-- and start selling clothes already, you know. And somehow, we survived-- until my father had to go with my mother for surgery to Krakow. He got trouble with [NON-ENGLISH], which got the stones in kidney. So we sent them over to Krakow. And a few weeks later, there was a clean-out the town-- which I put my brother, put my mother-in-law, and another kid with the Polish people, who promised me they're going to keep it until I'd be able--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest. Which he promised me, I'll be over there three, four days to pick him up. In the meantime, after we passed through the town, he-- not by the German. He's got something. And they put him over there to another town, Dembitz, which the railroad-- they were waiting for them. And sent them who knows? Treblinka, Belsen, I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your youngest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where were your other two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other two brothers-- one was taken to a-- a labor camp in Melitz, which was not too far from [NON-ENGLISH], over there. After eight months, I--that's what the people told me. They found out he's got-- the Germans are checking the barracks. That he's got more bread than he's supposed to have, and they took him out and shot him. The other one, I don't know the rest-- I think he died. My father-in-law, my wife's brother, died over there, too, in this camp. And he had a heart attack. And one of the brothers, my brother-in-law, survived. He was sent later from this camp to Dachau, and he survived. And I met him in Krakow after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? You went to Krakow to see where your parents were? What made you go to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: When, after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no, in 1943, you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Krakow to see my thing. And after they cleaned the town, I couldn't stay anymore. So I went to Krakow over there. Somehow I got a hold of people who let me in. I couldn't get into the ghetto. And I saw my parents, you know. And I got papers from people who knew me, that I can stay in Krakow. And I then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was you wife with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all the time. Then I got the first time in touch with Madritsch company-- which he opened a big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Can I just ask you when you got into the ghetto, what were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Waiting over there, and watching for the squealers or collaborators. They're going to recognize me or they're going to ask me, hey, where's your papers? Until I got the papers, it was fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a resistance or an underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know about it. Not that I know. I heard about it but I see things that I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what went through your mind when you went into the ghetto? How had the situation changed from when you were there before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one thing. I got the one thing in my life-- I never gave up. And that's what I think kept me alive. I never think, oh, I never go, live through this war. I always say, I'm going to live through. And never bothered my head over what I'm going to do next, this. Every day was a different day, and I knew I'm going to-- I talked myself into I'm going to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how had things changed? How had the situation changed from when you last been in Krakow to when you went back and there was a ghetto and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Listen, you haven't got time to think about it. You take as it is. You came, you saw people, squeezed three families in one room, you know. Like they say, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you sharing with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sharing with my wife. I moved-- I was sharing with my wife, with some kind of relatives who helped me to get to Madritsch. He was a tailor before the war, and with a big business. How you call this-- [GERMAN] they call this in German. He put me over there, and taught me how to cut goods. And I became the number one cutter. And my wife used to work over there, too, as a dressmaker, a seamstress. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Madritsch get started?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came over there and he got one of the businesses in Krakow, which before the war produced ready made garments. He took this over. And the owner was taking care of it-- a very nice guy. And he, through connections, too, he opened a big, big [GERMAN], working for the German government-- military, private things, you know, labor [NON-ENGLISH], you know. And we got there with 1,000 people on day shift, and 1,000 people on night shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish or some Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. This was already from ghetto, we used to go, because the building was outside. And then when they liquidated the ghetto, in Plaszow concentration camp, they got the barracks over there. We used to work the machine and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe Madritsch to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very handsome man. If I'd be a woman, I would fall in love with him. Very nice, polite, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friendly. Always ask questions if you need something. I remember once, I was working in the outside. We used to end of the day go back to the concentration camp. And I got sick. I don't know what happened. I got fever. He came to me, he says, listen, stay here. I take care. You don't have to go upstairs to the camp. And I was three days after the fever came, ask me if I need something, and this, you know. We got doctors here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came because I was a cutter. And he knew, Henry cuts, and they'll be plenty of work for everybody. So that's one example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were already in the camp. He used to send 2,000 breads, loaf breads to the camp for people. And we used to even give people outside-- other people. And then other things, you know. Even money we got, because we used to cut piece goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's say you got three yards for a uniform. We've got experienced cutters or pattern makers who made from 2 yards and 90 centimeters. So the 10 were left over. And after a thousand, thousand, thousand, we got yards and yards left over.

speaker: interviewee
text: He helped us send this to the city, and sell it. So we get money, and buy other things, you know. He used a bring in his car, even white bread, bologna, everything. But- not for everybody, because he couldn't. But-- and nobody was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Germans think of him of him doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. He start learning from Schindler. Bribe them, given in to them. He made uniforms for them free. They come over there-- big shots, you know, from the government-- to Madritsch, and that's the reason. In there, there were parties, buy them gift. So he-- we were saved. Nobody was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- did you come across any other Germans, German officers who came into your plant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the guy Goeth-- who came over there and he was bribed by Madritsch. They were friendly, too. He's got another assistant-- Madritsch. His name was Titsch-- who was more, all the time with us in camp. Something-- think, he was right away in front, nobody could touch us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody get injured or anything by German misbehaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not [INAUDIBLE]. I remember one situation, the Jewish police came over there in the morning. I don't know, they were mad about something. And they took us out from the barrack, and they exercise in the morning with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Madritsch came. He saw this. Says, what's this? He told them, you've got no business to be here. No right to come over here. And he report them to Goeth. They put them right away in the-- in the-- how you call this? The breaking stones. Usually, after two days you're dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: He find out what the punishment is, right away he run over there. Take him out, I don't want him to be killed. A few people he took from the grave before they were shot out.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Madritsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Madritsch, and Titsch. They were both, you know. But he wasn't in-- because Madritsch was mostly outside, and getting business, you know, dealing this. And Titsch was all the time-- he's got a room. One of the barracks over there is his office, and he's taking care of everybody. Then when we came over there, and the Russian were already near Krakow to empty the camp. So he came, it started with Schindler. He's going to move his people to Brunnlitz in Czechoslovakia. So Madritsch came over there to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, listen, who wants to go with Schindler? So I take care of this. And about 15 families-- I mean families, I mean wives, kids, sometimes. They went to Schindler, because he paid them, or something. I don't know. Can we just back a little bit? While you were in the ghetto and working for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Madritsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madritsch. What were the conditions in the ghetto like? Well, like I told you, you got four families living in one room. It's separated by blankets, so little privacy. And if you got money, you could buy anything. Smoke that, you know. Because outside the ghetto, you-- people-- Polish people-- came up with and they sold you-- selling this food, flower, whatever it is through the window, you know? So it was an open ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The windows you can see over there. So we threw them the money, they threw back the things, you know. Then there were people who went outside the ghetto, too, for work. So everybody was smuggling sometime under his clothing and pockets over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Judenrat do in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrat was something who has to take orders from German, and did what the German want them to do. Nothing was-- they got no power.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they good people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were bad people. Not everybody, but you know, whoever got the-- there was a Jewish police. They got uniforms. Once they put the uniform on, they were thinking, everybody get killed during the war, and they would be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, there was the other way. You know what I mean? There was a few-- few guys who were bad guys. In the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Police, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Judenrat was the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the one friend-- no friend. He went to school with me. He was a collaborator. And he went outside the ghetto all day searching for faces, Jewish faces. And he squealed, and they took them right away, and they brought them to the ghetto. His name was Arthur Leffler. And finally I think in Mauthausen, I think they killed him over there, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while the ghetto was going on after the Nazi occupation, did they have shuls? Did you they have-- did you observe Shabbat and Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe private somewhere over there. But there was no-- no time for this. Because you work seven days a week all over, you know. We came home, we were tired, looking for food, and that's it. Next day, the same thing. There were some before I came to the ghetto, they got some restaurants, nightclubs-- you know, before. But this-- everything was liquidated as the war progressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came back into the ghetto, how many people have they taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were taken away every week, every two weeks. I don't know how much. Because like I say, I came in 1943, which I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning? When in 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left this [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in middle of summer, I think. Yeah, middle of summer. And there were one, they called this Aussiedlung, which took a lot of people, mostly elderly. Then next one it was October, when I lost my parents, and my wife's parents. This was October in 1943. In 19-- in November, they liquidated the whole ghetto. And we went, all of us, to Krakow-Plaszow concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in November 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're right. And we stay over there until 19-- November-- October, November 1944. When we moved through Gross Rosen to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they made the ghetto, were there any Christians that were inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have to leave over there. They gave them better accommodations outside the ghetto. And the whole thing was surrounded by a wall, and there was one entrance. But I wasn't there when they built the ghetto. I came already to the ghetto, but she maybe be able to tell you-- Irene, she was longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody feel that things were just so bad that it was futile, that they wanted to commit suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I even got one of my relatives who, when in concentration camp, commit suicide. He was a teacher, a religion teacher. And he commit suicide in Krakow-Plaszow. There were a lot of people. Listen, he saw things over there, what this guy Goeth did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe anything that Goeth did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll give you an example. We came in the morning on the appellplatz. You know, appellplatz, they count everybody is there. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this every day you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, and every time we came back from work. They count us to see if nobody run away. Even if somebody died in the barrack, you have to bring him on the appellplatz. You lay that person.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the ghetto? Still in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Plaszow. In the ghetto, we didn't have a thing. But I'll give you an example from Krakow-Plaszow. In the morning, we were staying, you know, winter, summer we were in the pajamas, you know, striped. And they were counting, you know, every barrack. They had one guy who count you, and report it to the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Goeth came over there. And he came over there, you know, everybody was shivering. He was about 6' 5', 6' 6'. I'm sure you-- somebody can describe him over there. And he went to one guy and ask him, how come you didn't shave? He says, there was no water. The water was closed. That's what they did. Winter, there would water running, so water freezes. You go and slip, you break a leg, and it was OK. So he says, you tell me there was no water in this camp? Took this gun from the thing, and shot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him was one of my relatives, my father's cousin. His name was Birma. He saw this here, with the tall guy, he start losing consciousness.

speaker: interviewer
text: He asked to me, what's the matter with you? You don't like it? He put the gun over there on forehead, and shot him, too. This was, you know, good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were about 15,000 people staying over there watching this. Because this, at that time, there was no law-- the German later changed the law-- you cannot kill somebody in concentration until you get the upper approval from Berlin. You have to-- this was after, you know, outside, and the Red Cross start searching, asking questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they have to report over there the reason why. So there was one scene over there when they killed this guy from-- who was chief of the police, who was Goeth's right hand. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were liquidating. He got an order, Goeth, to finish off this Chilowitch because he knew too much. So Goeth says, no, I know him, and I trust him. He's never going to squeal. So they said, we can prove it to you. Other-- other Germans in camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did, they set up one of the Ukrainian guards to sell him a gun so he can run away from the camp. He went with this. And I remember, you know, he was with us-- came in and he ask us for money. We gave him money, we gave him material for Goeth, you know. He came one day, he says, listen, I need some money. I said, we haven't got because we didn't have contact with the outside because they closed the camp for a few days. So he went to his brother-in-law, which was our they called it-- policeman. OD, he used to bring us to-- from barracks to the place where we worked, and brought us back. You know, with the uniform there. He went to ask him, outside thing, you know. He gave him some money. And he was observed already by the German. Two days later, I go in the morning and I asked him-- you know, because I rescued his wife one day. I, you know, I run away from a transport already, and she followed me, and I brought her to ghetto with me. I went that thing. That's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked him, what's the matter with you? You feel like you're freezing? No, no, no. I got something on my mind. And this was the day he prepared to run away from camp. How? They were bringing stuff to ghetto, and outside the ghetto. And he is supposed to be this Ukrainian who sold him the gun, told him that, I take care of it. I bring you this day. I will be waiting for you over there then. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: About two hours later-- it was warm already-- I went to the barracks over there and I hear rumors. We used to have always each barrack got two entrances. On each entrance was a young fellow who used to watch if a German doesn't come. All of a sudden, he starts screaming, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, Goeth is approaching. Everybody, you know, what the hell is he doing during the day. He came to our barrack. We were the first barrack in the road over there. He knew because he come over there-- I need this, I need this, I need material, I need this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Schnitzer? This is a guy, Chilovitch's brother-in-law-- the guy who gave him money. So he says, yeah, he's in the barrack over there. So he called him. He called him. He came over there. And I remember he used to tie up his uniform. Where is your brother-in-law, Chilowitch? He said, I don't know. You don't know? All right. Turn over. He shot him right on the spot-- right in front of everything. Where's your wife? So he called the wife. She came, saw him-- him laying down already. She fell on him. He killed her, too. Chilovitch's wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wife, too. Yeah. Which was Chilovitch's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was that other guy's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his-- yeah, the Schnitzer, I think. Then, I remember he went inside over there and called. I was in the front. He says, listen, I know you people got money. And the money, and the visa,  and you know. And [NON-ENGLISH]-- If the money wasn't returned, we'd be all shot like this one. And Goeth is-- he uses the word, shit out from here. He went downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now we lay there. They took us all down and there was on the road, laying Chilowitch dead, his wife, his nephew, Schnitzer, his wife, about eight, 10 guys, with a big sign that they tried to-- you know how this, why he shot Chilowitch?

speaker: interviewee
text: He called them in the morning. Hey, listen, you got something to tell me, tell me. He wanted to get from him that he was planning to run away. He tried to help him because he was the guy who made money for him. He says, no, nothing. Nothing. Everything is fine. So he says, take off your boot. He took off his boot, and a gun fell out.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told this to Chilowitch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Take off. Because he knew already because--

speaker: interviewer
text: He told Chilowitch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the other guy tried to prove it to Chilowitch, you can trust him. So the gun fell out, and he says, you see? You told me only lies. How you are a soldier in Polish army. The gun is no good. And the where is the-- you've got three bundles of diamonds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chilowitch-- Goeth find out in the thing hidden diamonds, you know. So right away they took him.
